title: Add opt_balance_tree pass
body:
This pass converts cascaded chains of arithmetic and logic cells ($add, $mul, $and, $or, $xor) into balanced binary trees to improve timing performance in hardware synthesis. For example, converting sth like `a + b + c + d + e + f` into into balanced binary trees (`((a + b) + (c + d)) + ((e + f) + ...)`) cuts the critical path depth roughly in half.

The pass does a BFS traversal starting from each cell to find chains of the same operation type ($add, $mul, $and, $or, $xor). For each chain:

  1. Walk backwards from the sink to collect all source signals, checking that we only traverse cells of the matching type
  2. Verify all operands have consistent signedness (mixed signed/unsigned chains are skipped)
  3. Recursively build a balanced binary tree by splitting sources at the midpoint
  4. Replace the original chain with the tree

Width calculations handle the fact that adders grow by 1 bit per level and multipliers sum their input widths. The pass preserves all cell attributes and properly handles port connections.

**Command-line flags**: `-arith` for just arithmetic ops, `-logic` for just logic ops, or default for all supported types.

## Tests

  The test suite has 30 cases covering:
  - Basic chains (4-input AND, 8-input ADD)
  - Chains with intermediate outputs and fanout
  - Mixed bit widths and signedness checking
  - Pre-existing partial trees
  - Wide operands (64+ bits)
  - Diamond patterns and reconvergent fanout

Each test uses `equiv_opt` to verify functional equivalence and `select` with traversal operators to check the actual tree depth.

Run with: `./yosys tests/opt/opt_balance_tree.ys

Original implementation by Akash Levy <akash@silimate.com> for Silimate. Upstreamed from https://github.com/Silimate/yosys
